short.headline: India runs on coal: 70.8% of electricity, and consumption is still growing
short.dek: In 2025, coal generated 70.8% of India's electricity. But the real story is more than one number: absolute coal use keeps rising, imports matter, and the climate cost is growing.
short.body: India is deeply dependent on coal. In 2025, coal fuelled 70.8% of the country's electricity, producing 1,474.15 TWh out of a total 2,082.82 TWh. That share has edged down as clean sources like solar and wind have grown, but in absolute terms coal generation continues to climb. Beyond power, total coal consumption across all uses has surged from 1.82 quadrillion Btu in 1980 to 21.8 in 2024. Most of that coal is mined at home (1.01 million thousand metric tons in 2024), but imports have risen sharply too, reaching 2,32,811.51 thousand metric tons. This dependence comes at a heavy climate price: coal and coke emitted 2,117.06 million metric tonnes of CO₂ in 2024, a twelve-fold increase from 1980.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: Think of India's electricity like a big thali. The largest roti on that plate is coal, and it isn't getting smaller. Even though we are adding solar panels and wind turbines at speed, the country's hunger for power is growing so fast that coal consumption keeps going up every year. Coal is not just for electricity; industries like steel and cement also use it, as reflected in the total coal consumption number. This scale is not about blame; it is about the size of the challenge. The data shows coal use is expanding across sectors, so reducing dependence will take sustained effort.
macha.soWhat: India's coal use is not shrinking, it is expanding across all sectors, making the clean-energy race a marathon, not a sprint.

article.title: How dependent is India on coal?
article.standfirst: In 2025, coal generated 70.8% of India's electricity. But the real story is more than one number: absolute coal use keeps rising, imports matter, and the climate cost is growing.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How much of India’s electricity comes from coal?

In 2025, coal-fired power plants produced 1,474.15 terawatt-hours (TWh) of electricity. That was 70.82% of all electricity generated in India, which totalled 2,082.82 TWh. A terawatt-hour is a huge unit, one TWh is a trillion watt-hours. Think of it as the output of a 1,000-megawatt power plant running for about 41 days. Coal’s share in the generation mix is the most direct measure of dependence. Fossil fuels together supplied 73.35%, with gas adding just 2.33%. Clean sources, including hydro, nuclear, solar, and wind, made up the remaining 26.65%. While coal’s share edged down from somewhat higher levels earlier in the century, it remains utterly dominant. This single fuel still generates more than seven of every ten units of electricity that Indians use.

## How can coal’s share fall while total coal generation keeps rising?

A falling share can be deceiving. The absolute amount of coal-fired electricity has not dropped; it has grown. In 2025, coal plants generated 1,474.15 TWh, a number that, as the chart reveals, has risen over the years. What has changed is that clean generation grew even faster. Solar and wind additions, in particular, expanded total supply so rapidly that coal’s slice of a much larger pie got smaller, even though the slice itself got larger. In 2025, clean sources produced 554.81 TWh. The gap between coal and clean generation is still huge, but the trend matters: if electricity demand keeps climbing fast, coal output can rise for years even as its percentage share declines. The chart shows this dynamic, coal is not fading; it is being overtaken in growth by clean energy only in relative terms.

## Where does India get its coal?

Almost all the coal India burns comes from within its own borders. In 2024, domestic production stood at 1.01 million thousand metric tons. That is a huge leap from 1,10,224.87 thousand metric tons in 1980. Yet imports have grown even faster, from just 550 thousand metric tons in 1980 to 2,32,811.51 thousand metric tons in 2024. Though domestic output dwarfs imports, the imported portion is not trivial, it has grown by a factor of over 400 since 1980. The chart plots both lines, making the trend clear: India is producing more coal than ever, but it is also buying more abroad, adding a layer of import exposure to its coal dependence.

## How much coal does India use beyond electricity?

The electricity sector is the biggest burner of coal, but it is not the only one. Steelmaking, cement production, and other industries consume large volumes. The total energy consumption from coal, across all uses, reached 21.8 quadrillion British thermal units (Btu) in 2024. That is twelve times the 1.82 quadrillion Btu consumed in 1980. While Btu is an unfamiliar unit (one quadrillion Btu is enough to power about 10 million Indian homes for a year), the direction is what matters: total coal use has risen relentlessly. This broader coal footprint means that even if power-sector coal plateaus, overall dependence could persist. The chart shows this long climb, reminding us that coal is not just about electricity.

## What is the climate cost of India’s coal use?

Coal is the most carbon-intensive fossil fuel. In 2024, India’s coal and coke consumption emitted 2,117.06 million metric tonnes of CO₂, up from 175.18 million metric tonnes in 1980. That twelve-fold rise tracks the growth in coal use. Within the power sector alone, coal accounted for 95.92% of emissions, or 1,338.09 million metric tonnes out of total power-sector emissions of 1,394.95 million metric tonnes in 2025. The line in the chart rises steeply, a direct consequence of India’s coal dependence. For climate, the scale of this dependence means that even rapid renewable additions will struggle to bend the emissions curve downwards quickly.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India's electricity is overwhelmingly coal-powered: in 2025 coal made up 70.8% of generation. But dependence goes beyond the share, total coal use across all sectors, including steel and cement, has risen twelve-fold since 1980, driving a massive increase in CO2 emissions.

pullQuotes.quote: In 2025, coal supplied 70.82% of India's electricity, producing 1,474.15 TWh out of 2,082.82 TWh.
Coal and coke CO2 emissions hit 2,117.06 million metric tonnes in 2024, a twelve-fold rise since 1980.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Coal and coke CO2 emissions, latest

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: One terawatt-hour is a trillion watt-hours. Imagine a 1,000-megawatt power plant, like a big coal unit, running at full capacity for about 41 days; that produces roughly one TWh. It is the standard large unit for measuring annual electricity generation. A TWh does not tell you how many homes are lit; it tells you the total electrical energy produced.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All generation numbers on this page are in TWh, so we can compare coal with hydro, solar, and other sources directly.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Reliance on coal as the main fuel for making electricity or running industries. It is measured by looking at the share of coal in the mix, the absolute amount of coal burned, and how much of that coal is imported. A country can be dependent even if its coal share is falling, provided the total amount keeps rising.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This page answers exactly this question, examining dependence from several angles.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The portion of a country's coal supply that comes from abroad. If many coastal power plants or steel mills rely on foreign coal because local mines cannot supply the right grade, then sudden global price spikes or shipping disruptions can hit the economy. A high import share adds a vulnerability layer to dependence.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Understanding how much coal India imports is a key part of understanding its overall coal dependence.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The amount of carbon dioxide released for every unit of electricity generated, usually measured in grams of CO₂ per kilowatt-hour (gCO₂/kWh). Coal has the highest carbon intensity among fossil fuels, meaning it produces the most CO₂ per unit of energy. A high-carbon-intensity grid means that using more electricity directly adds more emissions.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: While not charted directly, the concept underlies the link between coal dependence and emissions: coal's high carbon intensity means that more coal automatically means higher CO₂ output.

visualizationDecisions.bestChartType: share strip or single stacked bar
visualizationDecisions.timeWindow: full history but emphasize latest point
visualizationDecisions.reason: A simple share visual is immediately readable on mobile and directly answers the headline question.
A line chart is best for showing trends in absolute TWh over time, with sparse ticks for mobile.
A multi-line chart allows comparison of production and import trends in one view.
A single-series line clearly communicates the long-term growth in total coal consumption.
A line chart of emissions over time plainly shows the climate impact trajectory.

chartExplainers.title: How much of India's electricity is coal
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2025, coal supplied 70.82% of India’s electricity, generating 1,474.15 TWh out of 2,082.82 TWh.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart displays the share of coal in India’s annual electricity generation over the full available period. The most recent year shows a coal share of 70.82%, down from higher levels in earlier decades but still commanding. Electricity generation is measured in terawatt-hours (TWh), a unit that counts each unit of electrical energy produced. A high share means that for every 100 units of electricity you use at home, about 71 come from coal. The rest comes from hydro (8.55%), nuclear (2.59%), solar, wind, and gas. The visual makes it easy to see that coal, while slowly losing some share, remains the backbone.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the primary measure of coal dependence in the power sector.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the percentage label for the latest bar and follow the share line across years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse a small drop in share with coal becoming unimportant, it still dominates.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: The share label is large and readable even on small screens.

chartExplainers.title: Coal generation keeps rising
chartExplainers.takeaway: While coal's share dips, the absolute amount of coal-fired electricity has kept increasing, reaching 1,474.15 TWh in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart tracks the actual terawatt-hours of electricity generated from coal (orange line) and from clean sources (green line) each year. It reveals that even as the percentage share of coal declined, the TWh output rose steadily. In 2025, clean generation reached 554.81 TWh, which is growing faster than coal, but coal is still more than double that figure. Because total electricity demand jumps every year, both lines can go up while the share shifts. The visual answers the puzzle: more renewables do not automatically mean less coal in absolute terms, they mean coal’s portion shrinks relative to a bigger whole.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It resolves the paradox of falling share with rising tonnage.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the two trend lines; if the green line rises faster than the orange one, coal's share drops even if both increase.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming that a falling share means coal generation is going down, it isn't.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Two distinct colours and sparse year labels keep the lines clear on phone screens.

chartExplainers.title: Coal: home production vs imports
chartExplainers.takeaway: India mines most of its own coal (1.01 million thousand metric tons in 2024), but imports have grown to 2,32,811.51 thousand metric tons.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines show domestic coal production and coal imports over time, both in thousand metric tons. Domestic output has climbed steeply from 1,10,224.87 thousand metric tons in 1980 to over a million in 2024. Imports, though much smaller in absolute terms, have risen even more dramatically, from just 550 thousand metric tons in 1980 to 2,32,811.51 thousand metric tons in 2024. The data does not reveal why imports have grown so fast, but the contrast with domestic production is clear. The gap between the lines remains large, but the trend shows a growing role for foreign coal in India's supply.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It highlights import exposure as a dimension of dependence.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the scale of the two lines and notice the steep rise in imports relative to their starting point.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Thinking imports are negligible, while small relative to production, they have grown by a factor of 400 and are an important part of the supply.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: The two lines are spaced well apart, so they are easy to distinguish even on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: Total coal use, beyond just power
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's total coal consumption reached 21.8 quadrillion Btu in 2024, a twelve-fold rise from 1.82 quadrillion Btu in 1980.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart shows the total primary energy consumption from coal, measured in quadrillion British thermal units. It captures all the coal burned, for power, steel, cement, and any other industrial use. The unit is large and unfamiliar: one quadrillion Btu is roughly the energy in 28 billion litres of petrol. The trend since 1980 is unmistakably upward. The 2024 value of 21.8 quadrillion Btu highlights that coal’s role extends well beyond the power sector. Even if India were to stop using coal for electricity tomorrow, industrial demand would keep the fuel in play.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It widens the lens from electricity to the full economy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Follow the line upward; the height at the end tells you the latest total coal burn.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Not confusing this with electricity generation, it includes all energy forms from coal.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single line with yearly ticks is very mobile-friendly.

chartExplainers.title: The CO₂ cost of coal
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal and coke emissions hit 2,117.06 million metric tonnes CO₂ in 2024, a near-twelve-fold jump from 175.18 million tonnes in 1980.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart traces the annual carbon dioxide emissions from the combustion of coal and coke in India. The line rises in lockstep with coal consumption: from 175.18 million metric tonnes in 1980 to 2,117.06 million metric tonnes in 2024. The power sector is the chief culprit, responsible for 1,338.09 million tonnes of that in 2025, about 95.92% of its own emissions. Coal is the most carbon-intensive fossil fuel, so the steep rise in emissions mirrors the growth in coal use. Any discussion of decarbonisation must confront this line.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It directly links coal dependence to climate change.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Observe the slope; a steeper rise means faster emissions growth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Thinking this is total national emissions, it is only from coal and coke.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single clear line with large numbers for the endpoints.

sourceNotes: Ember, yearly electricity generation data by source.
U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), total coal consumption, production, imports, and CO2 emissions from coal and coke.
caveats: Coal's share in electricity is a snapshot; absolute generation can rise even as share falls.
Total coal consumption includes non-electricity uses that are harder to displace with renewables.
CO2 data covers only coal and coke, not all greenhouse gases or other pollutants.

Total energy consumption from coal, earliest
Coal and coke CO2 emissions, latest
Coal and coke CO2 emissions, earliest

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India depends heavily on coal: in 2025 coal supplied 70.8% of electricity generation, absolute coal generation continues to rise, imports meet a fifth of demand, and total coal consumption across all sectors has grown 12-fold since 1980, making coal the single largest driver of the country’s CO₂ emissions.
requiredConcepts: Define electricity generation mix and the unit terawatt-hour (TWh).
Explain the share of coal in total generation versus the absolute amount of coal-fired electricity.
Explain coal production and imports, and why imports have risen over time.
Explain total coal consumption across all sectors (steel, cement, etc.), not just power.
State the most important caveat: a falling share does not mean falling absolute coal use; dependence must be assessed on multiple dimensions.

sectionPlan.heading: How much of India’s electricity comes from coal?
sectionPlan.job: Teach the headline measure of dependence: coal's share of total electricity generation.
conceptsToExplain: TWh as a unit of electricity generation
sectionPlan.caveat: A high share does not by itself reveal whether absolute coal generation is rising or falling; both trends matter.

sectionPlan.heading: How can coal’s share fall while total coal generation keeps rising?
sectionPlan.job: Explain the paradox of a falling share but rising absolute coal generation, driven by rapid growth in total electricity demand.
conceptsToExplain: how clean energy additions can reduce coal's share even as coal output increases
sectionPlan.caveat: Share is a snapshot of the mix; absolute generation shows the physical scale of coal use.

sectionPlan.heading: Where does India get its coal?
sectionPlan.job: Show the split between domestic production and imports, illustrating the import-exposure dimension of dependence.
conceptsToExplain: thousand metric tons as a unit of coal mass
sectionPlan.caveat: Import volumes are large but still a minority of total supply; however, imported coal is often critical for specific coastal power plants and industries.

sectionPlan.heading: How much coal does India use beyond electricity?
sectionPlan.job: Quantify total coal consumption across all sectors, showing that the scale of dependence is larger than the power sector alone.
lockedNumbersToUse: Total energy consumption from coal, latest
Total energy consumption from coal, earliest
conceptsToExplain: quadrillion Btu as a unit of total energy consumption
non-power uses of coal (steel, cement, other industry)
sectionPlan.caveat: Quadrillion Btu is a large, unfamiliar unit; focus on the trend rather than the absolute value.

sectionPlan.heading: What is the climate cost of India’s coal use?
sectionPlan.job: Quantify CO₂ emissions from coal and coke, linking coal dependence to its climate impact.
lockedNumbersToUse: Coal and coke CO2 emissions, latest
Coal and coke CO2 emissions, earliest
conceptsToExplain: MtCO₂ as a unit of emissions
why coal is the most carbon-intensive fossil fuel
sectionPlan.caveat: This chart covers only CO₂ from coal and coke, not total greenhouse gas emissions or other pollutants.

chartPlan.title: How much of India's electricity is coal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It directly answers the page question by showing the share of electricity generation that comes from coal.
chartPlan.howToRead: Look at the percentage share of coal in the latest year and see how it has changed over the full period.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming that a high share means coal is the only story; the mix contains other important sources like hydro and renewables.

chartPlan.title: Coal generation keeps rising
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It reveals that even if coal's share falls, the absolute amount of coal-fired electricity is still climbing.
chartPlan.howToRead: Track both the coal line and the clean-sources line in TWh; notice whether the gap is narrowing or widening.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Interpreting a falling share as a sign that coal generation is declining; absolute values can rise even as the share drops.

chartPlan.title: Coal: home production vs imports
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It adds the import dimension: India produces most of its own coal but still relies on substantial imports.
chartPlan.howToRead: Compare the domestic production line with the imports line; note the scale difference and the trend over time.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Thinking imports are negligible because production is much larger; in some years imports have grown rapidly and are vital for certain users.

chartPlan.title: Total coal use, beyond just power
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It shows that coal’s role extends far beyond electricity, feeding steel mills, cement plants, and other industries.
chartPlan.howToRead: Observe the overall upward trend in quadrillion Btu; note that the line includes all coal burned, not just power generation.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Forgetting that the power sector is not the only consumer; discussions of coal dependence must account for industrial use too.

chartPlan.title: The CO₂ cost of coal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It links coal dependence directly to its climate impact, making clear why the scale of reliance matters.
chartPlan.howToRead: Follow the line in million metric tonnes of CO₂; see how it has grown sharply since 1980.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Confusing this with total emissions; this is only coal and coke, not oil, gas, or other sources.

dataSelectionAudit.reason: The selected data points cover the essential dimensions of coal dependence: electricity share, absolute generation, import dependency, total consumption, and emissions. Together they answer the page question from multiple angles.

visualizationDecisions.bestChartType: share strip or single stacked bar
visualizationDecisions.timeWindow: full history but emphasize latest point
visualizationDecisions.reason: A simple share visual is immediately readable on mobile and directly answers the headline question.
A line chart is best for showing trends in absolute TWh over time, with sparse ticks for mobile.
A multi-line chart allows comparison of production and import trends in one view.
A single-series line clearly communicates the long-term growth in total coal consumption.
A line chart of emissions over time plainly shows the climate impact trajectory.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A unit of electricity equal to one trillion watt-hours. If a 1,000 MW power plant runs for one hour, it produces 1,000 megawatt-hours, and 1,000 such hours make a TWh.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: All electricity generation numbers on this page are in TWh, allowing us to compare coal with other sources.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Reliance on coal as the main fuel for electricity, industry, or overall energy. It is measured by the share of coal in the mix, the absolute amount used, and the share of imports.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: This page answers exactly this question, examining dependence from several angles.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The portion of a country’s coal supply that comes from abroad. A high import share can make a country vulnerable to global price swings and supply disruptions.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Understanding how much coal India imports is a key part of understanding its overall coal dependence.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The amount of CO₂ emitted per unit of electricity produced, measured in grams of CO₂ per kilowatt-hour (gCO₂/kWh). A higher number means dirtier electricity.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: While not charted directly, the concept underlies the link between coal dependence and emissions: coal has a high carbon intensity, so more coal means higher emissions.

proseRules: No clichés or AI tells (examine, pattern, etc.).
Use only numbers present in the lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
Do not perform any calculations unless the evidence packet provides the derived number.
Start every section with a reader-question H2 heading.

evidence.question: How dependent is India on coal?

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
